title: Cream puffs
subtitle: Small choux buns filled with whipped cream
excerpt: Cream puffs are small choux buns, baked hollow, split and filled with whipped cream or pastry cream. The choux is the same formula as eclairs; the difference is the round piped shape and the cream-only fill. The shell should be golden brown and dry right through, so it stays crisp for at least an hour after filling.
sourceNotes: Choux pastry (pâte à choux) is a French preparation documented in patisserie manuals from at least the seventeenth century. Small filled choux buns appear in English-language baking texts from the nineteenth century onward. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg, includes choux-based recipes. The cream puff as a distinct format is documented in American and British domestic cookery through the twentieth century.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the unfilled baked shells in a sealed bag. Refresh from frozen in a 180°C oven for 5 minutes and cool before filling.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bake and cool the shells up to a day ahead; keep in an airtight tin at room temperature. Fill close to serving.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; do not open the door in the first 20 minutes or the choux will collapse

content.text: Cream puffs are small, hollow choux buns filled with cream. The choux is made by cooking flour into boiling butter-and-water until the paste pulls away from the pan, then beating in eggs until the paste falls in a slow V from the spoon. Piped and baked at high heat, the steam inside puffs them hollow. They should be deep gold and dry inside; a pale choux bun is an underdone one.

Bring the water, butter, and salt to a full boil in a medium saucepan. Take the pan off the heat and tip in all the sifted flour at once. Stir vigorously with a wooden spoon until the paste pulls cleanly away from the sides of the pan and forms a smooth ball. Return to a low heat and cook the paste, stirring constantly, for 2 minutes. The purpose is to drive out moisture; the paste should be matte and firm.

Tip the paste into a bowl and leave to cool for 5 minutes. Add the beaten egg a little at a time, beating vigorously after each addition. The paste will look as though it has split; keep beating and it will come back together. Stop adding egg when the paste falls from a spoon in a slow, thick V shape and the surface looks glossy. You may not need all the egg.

Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Line two baking trays with baking paper. Spoon the paste into a piping bag fitted with a 1 cm round nozzle and pipe 20 mounds, about 4 cm across, spaced well apart. Dip a finger in water and press down any peaks. Bake for 28 to 30 minutes until deep golden brown and crisp. Do not open the oven in the first 20 minutes. The buns should feel hollow and light when tapped underneath. If still soft inside, return to the oven for 5 minutes with the door ajar. Cool completely on a wire rack.

Whip the cream with the icing sugar and vanilla to soft peaks. Split each bun at the equator or poke a hole in the base and pipe the cream in. Dust with icing sugar to serve. Fill within 2 hours of serving; the shells soften in the fridge.
